title: Linear, neighbor and tree models

sources.claim: Linear, nearest-neighbor, and tree models encode different assumptions about boundaries, scaling, locality, interactions, sample size, interpretability, and prediction cost; comparison holds data and metrics constant.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Linear, nearest-neighbor, and tree models encode different assumptions about boundaries, scaling, locality, interactions, sample size, interpretability, and prediction cost; comparison holds data and metrics constant.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Logistic regression, scaled k-nearest neighbors, and a depth-bounded tree use the same grouped folds and primary metric, with latency and slice errors reported. Your goal is to select and compare at least three model families using the same split and metric plan. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Data and experimental workflow; Conditional probability and Bayes rule; Regression; Classification and probabilities. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Bounded operational model
sections.body: Linear, nearest-neighbor, and tree models encode different assumptions about boundaries, scaling, locality, interactions, sample size, interpretability, and prediction cost; comparison holds data and metrics constant. Any capability or quality claim remains limited to an explicit task, population, conditions, baseline, and recorded evidence.

sections.heading: Failure boundary and non-claim
sections.body: No family is universally best, preprocessing changes comparisons, hyperparameter searches add selection bias, tree importance can mislead, and a more complex model must beat relevant baselines with uncertainty. The learner must reject this overclaim: A tree is always interpretable, nearest neighbors needs no training choices, and the model with the best validation score is objectively superior.

examples.title: Linear, neighbor and tree models: stress and transfer case
examples.situation: K-nearest neighbors degrades when irrelevant unscaled dimensions dominate distance, while a tree overfits small leaves unless complexity is controlled.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name the changed population, input, assumption, or failure: K-nearest neighbors degrades when irrelevant unscaled dimensions dominate distance, while a tree overfits small leaves unless complexity is controlled.

Step 2 — Keep the non-claim visible: No family is universally best, preprocessing changes comparisons, hyperparameter searches add selection bias, tree importance can mislead, and a more complex model must beat relevant baselines with uncertainty.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting overclaim: A tree is always interpretable, nearest neighbors needs no training choices, and the model with the best validation score is objectively superior.
